title: Living Stones and Submission
subtitle: A spiritual house, a royal priesthood, called to honorable conduct and submissive suffering

heb.paragraph: Living stones built into a spiritual house. Christ is the cornerstone (Isaiah 28:16); believers are stones built upon Him.
A royal priesthood - the entire community serves priestly functions. Access to God is not mediated by a special class.
A people for God's own possession - acquired, treasured, set apart. The language echoes Exodus 19:5.

refs.note: Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone, a cornerstone chosen and precious.
The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone.
You shall be my treasured possession, a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.
I will say to Not My People, You are my people.

notes.note: Put away all malice. Like newborn infants, crave pure spiritual milk that you may grow. You have tasted that the Lord is good.
Come to the living stone - rejected by men but chosen by God. You are living stones, a spiritual house, a holy priesthood.
You are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation. Once not a people, now God's people; once without mercy, now having received mercy.
Calvin notes the double function of Christ the stone: cornerstone to believers, stumbling block to the disobedient.
The corporate titles belong to the whole church, not a clerical subset. Every believer is priest; the entire community is royal.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase pure spiritual milk (logikon adolon gala) may mean rational/word-based milk - nourishment from the word of God.
Spiritual house (oikos pneumatikos) replaces the physical temple. The community is where God dwells.
The fourfold designation echoes Exodus 19:5-6 and Isaiah 43:20-21. Israel's titles transfer to the church.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes the corporate dimension: individual stones form a collective house; individual priests constitute a corporate priesthood.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes the social implications: these Gentile converts, marginalized in Asia Minor, are told they are the true chosen race.

hist.historical: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) — Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. These believers face social ostracism and verbal abuse (4:4, 14) rather than official state persecution. As 'elect exiles' (1:1), they experience the marginalization of those who no longer participate in pagan civic religion, trade guild feasts, and family worship. The letter's household codes (2:13–3:7) address how Christians should live within Roman social structures. Peter writes from 'Babylon' (5:13) — almost certainly a symbolic name for Rome. Silvanus (Silas) serves as secretary (5:12), and Mark is present (5:13). The letter likely dates to the early 60s AD, before Nero's persecution.
hist.context: Peter builds on chapter 1 by describing the community's corporate identity. Having put away malice, they crave spiritual milk for growth. Coming to Christ the living stone, they become living stones built into a spiritual house. Peter weaves OT texts: the cornerstone (Isaiah 28:16), the stone of stumbling (Isaiah 8:14), and the rejected stone that became chief (Psalm 118:22). The climax uses Exodus and Hosea language: you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation.

heb.paragraph: Keep your conduct honorable among the Gentiles - the term covers the whole pattern of life. Good conduct silences slander.
Be subject to every human institution - submission is the posture of Christ-followers in a hostile society.
Live as free people, but do not use freedom as a cover-up for evil. Christian freedom is not license but liberation for service.

refs.note: Let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father.
Let every person be subject to the governing authorities.
You were called to freedom, brothers. Only do not use your freedom as an opportunity for the flesh.
Fear the LORD and the king.

notes.note: Abstain from passions that wage war against your soul. Keep conduct honorable - that Gentiles may see good deeds and glorify God.
Be subject to every human institution for the Lord's sake - whether emperor or governors sent to punish evil and praise good.
This is God's will: that by doing good you silence the ignorance of foolish people. Honor everyone; love the brotherhood; fear God; honor the emperor.
Calvin emphasizes the apologetic purpose of good conduct. The world slanders Christians as evildoers; visible righteousness refutes the slander.
Submission is not approval of all government does but recognition of God-ordained order.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The day of visitation (hemera episkopes) could mean judgment or gracious visitation. Context suggests the latter.
The paradox of Christian freedom: truly free yet slaves of God. Autonomous freedom is slavery; slavery to God is true freedom.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that submission here is strategic, not absolute. Peter's concern is the church's reputation in a hostile culture.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes emphasizes that the readers were accused of being social deviants. Peter's strategy is visible virtue that proves accusations false.

hist.context: Peter shifts to ethical instruction for life in a pagan society. As sojourners and exiles, believers must abstain from fleshly passions. Their conduct must be honorable - so that slanderers will see good deeds and glorify God. Submission to human institutions (emperor, governors) is commanded - not because the state is righteous but because such submission silences critics.

sections.header: Verses 18-25 - "Slaves, Submit - Christ Suffered for You"

heb.paragraph: Household servants are to submit to masters - with all respect, not only to the good but also to the unjust.
Christ left you an example (hypogrammos) - the term refers to letters traced for children to copy. Christ's suffering is the pattern.
He bore our sins in his body on the tree - the cross is called tree as in Deuteronomy 21:23. Christ's substitutionary death is the basis for ethical transformation.

refs.note: He was pierced for our transgressions; by his wounds we are healed.
He had done no violence, and there was no deceit in his mouth.
Jesus remained silent before the high priest.
I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.

notes.note: Servants, submit with all respect - even to the unjust. Enduring unjust suffering while mindful of God is gracious.
Christ suffered for you, leaving an example. He committed no sin. When reviled, he did not revile; when suffering, he entrusted himself to the righteous Judge.
He bore our sins on the tree, that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. By his wounds you have been healed.
Calvin notes that unjust suffering is specifically gracious because it mirrors Christ. Suffering for wrong earns nothing; suffering for right echoes the cross.
Christ is both example (moral pattern) and substitute (bearing our sins). The two are inseparable.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term hypogrammos (example) is a hapax legomenon in the NT. It refers to a writing model children trace to learn letters.
The phrase bore our sins (anenegken) may echo the sacrificial language of Isaiah 53:12 LXX.
Shepherd and Overseer (poimen kai episkopos) are pastoral titles applied to Christ. He tends and supervises the flock.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that vv. 21-25 are among the most important Christological texts in the NT. Ethical exhortation is grounded in atonement theology.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes that Peter does not endorse slavery but addresses slaves within their situation. The focus is faithful witness within existing structures.

hist.context: Peter addresses household slaves - a significant population in the early church. They are to submit even to harsh masters, for this is gracious: to endure suffering unjustly while mindful of God. Christ is the example: He suffered for you, leaving a pattern to follow. The passage becomes a Christological hymn: He committed no sin, did not revile, entrusted Himself to the righteous Judge, bore our sins on the tree. By His wounds you have been healed.

lit.note: Chapter 2 develops corporate identity (living stones) into household ethics (submission). Christ's suffering becomes the paradigm.

themes.note: Covenant is strong in the royal priesthood language. Mission appears in the apologetic purpose of good conduct.
